name: I-V-vi-IV
description: The most widely used pop progression. Found in thousands of hit songs from the 1990s onward. Provides a satisfying, uplifting harmonic cycle.

name: I-IV-V-I
description: Fundamental three-chord progression. The backbone of rock, country, and folk music. Simple and universally recognizable.

name: ii-V-I
description: The essential jazz cadence. Nearly every jazz standard contains this progression. The strongest harmonic resolution in tonal music.

name: I-vi-IV-V (Doo-Wop)
description: Classic 1950s doo-wop changes. Also known as the '50s progression. Used in 'Stand by Me', 'Earth Angel', and countless others.

name: 12-Bar Blues
description: The standard 12-bar blues form. Foundation of blues, early rock and roll, and boogie-woogie. Each chord symbol represents one bar.

name: Andalusian Cadence
description: Descending minor cadence with Spanish and flamenco origins. Common in classical guitar, film scores, and Mediterranean folk music.

name: ii-V-I-vi (Jazz Turnaround)
description: Standard jazz turnaround used at the end of chorus sections. Creates a seamless loop back to the ii chord for the next chorus.

name: I-vi-ii-V (Rhythm Changes Bridge)
description: Circle-of-fifths progression from Gershwin's 'I Got Rhythm'. Basis for hundreds of jazz contrafacts and bebop compositions.

name: i-iv-v-i (Minor Blues)
description: Minor key blues progression. Dark, moody character used in minor blues, RnB ballads, and soul music.

name: vi-IV-I-V (Sensitive Female)
description: Rotation of the I-V-vi-IV starting on the minor chord. Creates a more melancholic, introspective feel. Common in 2000s pop and indie rock.

name: I-IVmaj7-iii7-vi7 (Neo-Soul)
description: Lush neo-soul progression with extended chords. Smooth, warm harmonic movement favored by artists like Erykah Badu and D'Angelo.

name: i-VII-VI-VII (Aeolian Vamp)
description: Natural minor oscillation between i and VII with VI as a passing chord. Common in modern RnB, hip-hop beats, and trap-soul.

name: Pachelbel Canon
description: The famous Pachelbel Canon chord sequence. Baroque origin but adapted extensively in pop, rock, and wedding music.

name: i-VII-VI-V (Harmonic Minor Descent)
description: Descending bass line through the natural minor scale with a major V chord. Dramatic and passionate, common in classical and film music.

name: I-bVII-IV-I (Mixolydian Vamp)
description: Mixolydian mode progression with the flat seventh. Rock and roots feel. Used by The Beatles, Tom Petty, and many classic rock artists.

name: iii-vi-ii-V (Circle of Fifths)
description: Extended circle-of-fifths movement. Creates strong forward harmonic motion. Fundamental to bebop and post-bop jazz harmony.

name: I-V-vi-iii-IV-I-ii-V (Extended Canon)
description: Extended 8-bar progression combining the Pachelbel Canon with a ii-V resolution. Smooth, flowing harmonic cycle for ballads.

name: i-bVI-bIII-bVII (Epic Minor)
description: Dramatic minor key progression using natural minor chords. Common in film scores, power ballads, and modern epic pop.

name: Imaj7-ii9-iii7-IVmaj7 (Smooth Jazz)
description: Stepwise ascending progression with lush extensions. Smooth, sophisticated sound used in smooth jazz and contemporary arrangements.

name: I-IV-I-V (Gospel Vamp)
description: Simple gospel and soul vamp. Energetic and uplifting. Foundation of gospel piano, Motown, and classic soul music.
